coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots derived from Mangifera indica leaves for selective detection of metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Jagpreet
Kaur, Sukhmeen
Lee, Jechan
Mehta, Akansha
Kumar, Sanjeev
Kim, Ki-Hyun
Basu, Soumen
Rawat, Mohit
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report an inexpensive, green, and one-pot synthesis method for highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using mango (Mangifera indica: M. indica) leaves to develop an efficient sensing platform for metal ions. The CQDs synthesized from M. indica leaves via pyrolysis treatment at 300 °C for 3 h were characterized by various spectroscopic and electron microscopy techniques for their structural, morphological, and optical properties. Accordingly, the synthesized CQDs showed an absorption peak at 213 nm to confirm the p–p* transition of the carbon core state, while the CQD particles were spherical with a size less than 10 nm. The prepared CQDs showed excellent fluorescent properties with blue emission spectra (around 525 nm) upon excitation at 435 nm. The synthesized CQDs had the prodigious sensing potential to detect Fe2+ ions in water with a limit of detection of 0.62 ppm. Additionally, their sensing capability was tested using a real sample (e.g., Livogen tablet). Moreover, the synthesized CQDs showed substantial stability over a long period (three months). Thus, this study provides an inexpensive and facile method for CQD-based sensing of Fe2+ ions with a photoluminescence quenching mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mangifera indica
CQDs
Sensing of Fe2+
Fluorescent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of M. indica leaf-derived CQDs
Detection of metal ions using CQDs
Physical and chemical properties of the CQDs derived from M. indica leaves
pH dependence of PL emission of the CQDs
Effect of Fe2+ ions on PL spectra of the CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by financial assistance from the Shromani Gurdwara Prabhandak Committee (SGPC), Amritsar. The authors are thankful to the Vice-Chancellor of SGGSW and Thapar University for providing laboratory facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectroscopic analysis of the synthesized CQDs: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, (b) PL emission spectra, (c) PL emission behavior of the CQDs as a function of wavelength, and (d) FTIR spectrum.
Morphological analysis: (a) HRTEM image of the CQDs, (b) HRTEM image of a single CQD, (c) XPS spectra of the CQDs, and (d) XPS scan of C 1s.

Sensing characteristics of the CQDs: (a) PL spectra of the CQDs as a function of pH, (b) selectivity of the CQDs toward different metal ions, (c) fluorescence spectra of the CQDs as a function of Fe2+ concentration, and (d) Fe2+ Stern-Volmer plot.
Fluorescence intensity of the CQDs as a function of increase in amount of Livogen tablet.
Schematics for the proposed CQD's sensing mechanism and their interaction with metal ions.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs between freshly prepared form vs. three months aging.
Comparison of performance between optical sensing systems for detection of iron ions.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots derived from Mangifera indica leaves for selective detection of metal ions

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report an inexpensive, green, and one-pot synthesis method for highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using mango (Mangifera indica: M. indica) leaves to develop an efficient sensing platform for metal ions. The CQDs synthesized from M. indica leaves via pyrolysis treatment at 300 °C for 3 h were characterized by various spectroscopic and electron microscopy techniques for their structural, morphological, and optical properties. Accordingly, the synthesized CQDs showed an absorption peak at 213 nm to confirm the p–p* transition of the carbon core state, while the CQD particles were spherical with a size less than 10 nm. The prepared CQDs showed excellent fluorescent properties with blue emission spectra (around 525 nm) upon excitation at 435 nm. The synthesized CQDs had the prodigious sensing potential to detect Fe2+ ions in water with a limit of detection of 0.62 ppm. Additionally, their sensing capability was tested using a real sample (e.g., Livogen tablet). Moreover, the synthesized CQDs showed substantial stability over a long period (three months). Thus, this study provides an inexpensive and facile method for CQD-based sensing of Fe2+ ions with a photoluminescence quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past three decades, there have been numerous efforts to apply fluorescent materials to optoelectronic, sensing, and bio-imaging devices, due to their high fluorescent quantum yield, interference free signal, etc. (Hirano et al., 2000; Li et al., 2015; Rasheed et al., 2019, 2018a, 2018b; Soroka et al., 1987; Tang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2016; Yoon et al., 2018). Among different fluorescent materials, the use of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) has been prevalent due to their high photostability, absolute photoluminescence quantum yields, and size-tunable absorption and emission (Hardman, 2006; Sun et al., 2006; Wu et al., 2003). Typical fluorescent materials in the form of QDs have been prepared from compounds of gold, silver, lead, silicon, and cadmium (Branham et al., 2006; Hutter and Maysinger, 2011; Jin and Gao, 2009). However, these typical QDs are disadvantageous because of their potential toxicity, posing adverse environmental health hazards (Manshian et al., 2017). Therefore, there has been a demand to develop a sustainable method to prepare stable and high intensity fluorescent QDs with high photo-stability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity.

In this respect, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon family that are recognized as promising alternative to replace conventional semiconductor quantum dots (Lim et al., 2015). Note that conventional QDs comprises of heavy metals which are extremely toxic even at comparatively low concentration. In this respect, CQDs may take a number of advantages such as low toxicity and biocompatibility along with common properties (e.g., high photoluminescence, low photodegradation, and high-water solubility). All of these properties are desired for effective sensing of metal ions and bio-imaging applications (Ju et al., 2018; Kalytchuk et al., 2017; Mehta et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2017).

During the electrophoretic analysis of single-walled carbon nanotube, fluorescent CQDs were serendipitously discovered (Xu et al., 2004). In their study, they observed that this carbon nanotube fragment can be segmented into a number of moieties with size-dependent fluorescent properties. This prompted the creation of CQDs as a new viable class of fluorescent nanomaterials to replace existing QDs. For the first time, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were recognized as “carbon quantum dots” (Sun et al., 2006). These authors proposed a new route for the synthesis of CQDs through surface modification that improved fluorescence emissions considerably (Sun et al., 2006). These studies reported that CQDs contain a high amount of diamond-type sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms along with graphite-type sp2 hybridization (Ray et al., 2009; Sun et al., 2006). Consequently, they can exhibit irregular π-electronic conjugation in the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Yang et al., 2011). Therefore, CQDs may contain a number of elementary constituents such as graphene, graphene oxide, and diamond. These exceptional structural properties of CODs indicate the existence of numerous fluorophores.

To date, the synthesis of CQDs can be carried out based on either top-down or bottom-up approaches. In the former route, CQDs are obtained by cutting or evaporating the bulk source of carbon into nanoparticles through physical processes, such as electrochemical methods (Liu et al., 2007), laser-ablation (Thongpool et al., 2012), plasma induction (Li et al., 2013), and arc evaporation (Dey et al., 2014). In contrast, in the bottom-up approach, CQDs are obtained by nucleation of individual atoms. This includes thermal methods (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009), solvothermal/hydrothermal methods (Liu et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2013), microwave treatment (Zhu et al., 2009), and ultrasonic-assisted synthetic methods (Li et al., 2011; Lu et al., 2016). However, they have many disadvantages, such as harsh synthesis conditions (e.g., high temperature, reactive chemical reactions), expensive precursors and apparatus, and long synthesis times. In addition, these methods involve toxic chemicals, such as strong acids and bases, that generate wastewater (Lim et al., 2015; Namdari et al., 2017; Wang and Hu, 2014). Therefore, there have been numerous efforts to develop alternate natural, non-toxic, and inexpensive materials as precursors for application toward a sustainable synthesis procedure for CQDs. For example, the synthesis of CQDs has been tested using grass (Liu et al., 2012), egg white (Cai et al., 2011), honey (Mandani et al., 2017), dairy waste (Devi et al., 2017), sweet potato (Wen et al., 2014), bamboo leaves (Liu et al., 2014), pomelo peel (Lu et al., 2012), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), tulsi, neem leaves (Meena et al., 2019), banana juice (Chaudhry et al., 2020), lemon juice (Hoan et al., 2019), and tea extract (Chen et al., 2019).

Iron is an indispensable micronutrient in our diets, with the human body requiring about 30 mg per day (Eid et al., 2017; He et al., 2008). It plays a momentous role in biological systems, such as in oxygen transport, binding with regulatory proteins, and enzyme activity. For example, it acts as a binding site to attract oxygen from breathed air to produce hemoglobin (Karami et al., 2010). However, an excess amount of iron ions may lead to serious health problems, such as cytotoxicity, Parkinson's disease, need for blood transfusions, and metabolic disorders. Further, free or excess iron ions can also result in damage of cellular systems or produce reactive oxygen species due to their tendency to accept or gain electrons from surroundings (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014; Britton et al., 2002; Eid et al., 2017; Puntarulo, 2005). Therefore, detection of iron ions in biological systems and the environment is of great concern.

This study attempted to prepare highly fluorescent CQDs using the renewable carbon source Mangifera indica: M. indica (mango) leaves. M. indica leaves were chosen as the carbon source of CQDs because of their abundance (mango is one of the most common fruits cultivated in India) (Mukherjee, 1953). The synthesized CQDs were employed for selective detection of Fe2+ ions in water as well as using a Livogen tablet (as a real sample to validate the efficacy of the study) over a wide range of pH values. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study on the synthesis of CQDs using mango leaves, which have high fluorescent and efficient sensing sensitivity toward Fe2+ ions. Moreover, the use of M. indica leaves extract for the synthesis of CQDs offers an extra benefit in terms of the excellent binding capability with Fe2+ ions (e.g., due to carboxylic/amine groups in such extract) (Masibo and Qian, 2008). This study should help pave the new road for the development of sustainable synthesis of a highly efficient sensing platform toward Fe2+ ions in water.

Mangifera indica (M. indica) leaves were collected from Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab, India, in July 2017. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), buffer solutions (pH from 1 to 11), and deionized (DI) water were purchased from Fisher Scientific, Emsure, Merck, and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of M. indica leaf-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The M. indica leaf-derived CQDs were synthesized as follows. (1) M. indica leaves were cleaned by washing with water and then chopped; (2) the chopped leaves were heated at 300 °C in a muffle furnace for 3 h to completely evaporate moisture; (3) the dried leaves were pulverized; (4) the powdered leaves (4 g) and H2O2 (5 mL) were added in 200 mL DI water; (5) the solution was stirred at ambient temperature for 12 h; and (6) the solution was then vacuum filtered to remove any precipitates and to obtain purified CQDs. The obtained CQDs showed bright blue luminescence under a UV illuminator.

The pH-dependent photoluminescence sensitivity of the synthesized CQDs was observed on a photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometer. The variation in PL intensity of dispersed CQDs was plotted against pH values of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions using CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the metal selectivity of synthesized CQDs, PL intensity was monitored as a function of wavelength for different metal ions at room temperature. All sensing experiments with different metal ions were performed under comparable experimental conditions (temperature, stirring speed, and retention time). One mL solutions of different metal ions (25 ppm) including diverse metal species (e.g., Cu2+, Al3+ Mg2+, K2+, Li2+, Na2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Sn2+, and Fe2+) were prepared. After that, 2 mL of the CQDs (as a control) were added into the prepared metal ion solutions, and the PL spectra of the CQD-added solutions were obtained using a photo-spectrometer. The maximum quenching in the PL intensity was observed with Fe2+ ions. Therefore, further sensing was conducted with solution containing 10 ppm Fe2+ ions.

The optical absorption spectrum of the synthesized CQDs was obtained using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). The spectrum was recorded in the wavelength range from 200 nm to 800 nm. Chemical bonding of the CQDs was analyzed using a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Alpha-T, Bruker). The spectrum was recorded at wavelengths ranging from 500 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1. The fluorescence intensity against the wavelength spectrum of CQDs was recorded using a photo-spectrometer (FLS980-S2S2-s, Edinburgh Instruments). Surface morphology (shape, size, and distribution of particle size) of the CQDs was characterized using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JE-2100, JEOL) at a voltage of 200 kV. The oxidation state of CQDs was investigated through X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) (KRATOS Axis 165 Shimadzu, UK) with Mg Ka radiation (1252.6 eV at 75 W).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of the CQDs derived from M. indica leaves
ce:section.ce:para: An optical absorption spectrum of the CQDs was recorded from 200 nm to 800 nm as shown in Fig. 1a. It showed a broad peak around 213 nm, which likely reflects the p–p* transition in the conjugated CC domain of the carbon-core state. According to the PL spectrum of the CQDs in Fig. 1b, a high-intensity emission peak around 525 nm was clearly visible. This massive blue light emission was likely due to differences in particle size distribution and light radiative state present on the surface of the synthesized CQDs. Blue emission is known to arise for two reasons. First, the occurrence of an intrinsic bandgap can lead to confinement of sp2 carbon conjugation in the core state of CQDs (Mehta et al., 2017). Second, extrinsic fluorescence might be due to surface dangling states of the CQDs, in which electrons are easily trapped either through direct or indirect (energy transfer from the intrinsic band) energy. Thus, blue emission of the CQDs depends upon both the intrinsic bandgap and extrinsic fluorescence.

The PL emission spectra of the CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 1c. The PL emission peak intensities varied from 485 nm to 550 nm with excitation wavelength ranging from 395 nm to 485 nm. An increase in excitation wavelength led to a red shift in peak intensity. Therefore, it could be concluded that the PL emission spectra of the CQDs depended rather sensitively on excitation wavelength.

Fig. 1d shows the FTIR spectra of the CQDs. The absorption peaks at 3673 cm−1 and 3614 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of carboxylic acid (–COOH) and –OH functional groups. The absorption peaks at 2928 cm−1 and 2859 cm−1 belonged to the vibration mode of the CH bond. The peaks at ~1744 cm−1 and 1916 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrational mode of CO and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the = C-H bond, respectively (Gu et al., 2018; Ramanan et al., 2018). The absorption peaks observed at 1629 cm−1, 1014 cm−1, and 1490 cm−1 should also reflect vibration of CC bonds, stretching vibration of COO– bonds, and bending vibrations of NH bonds, respectively (Mehta et al., 2017; Qu et al., 2013). Thus, due to the presence of hydrophilic groups (–OH and CO) as well as hydrophobic groups (CC and CH) on the surface, the CQDs exhibited an amphiphilic nature (Gu et al., 2018; Kaur et al., 2019).

Morphological characterization results of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 2a. The HRTEM characterization showed that aggregates of irregular spherical-shaped CQDs were formed with sizes ranging from 2 nm to 10 nm. The results also confirmed the presence of graphitic and amorphous carbon with lattice fringes of 2.2 Å and 1.7 Å (Fig. 2b). Elemental analysis of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2c. Three distinct XPS peaks at 284.52 eV, 401.7 eV, and 532.26 eV were observed in the XPS scan, accounting for the C 1s peak of carbon, N 1s peak of nitrogen, and O 1s peak of oxygen, respectively (Gu et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 2c, the CQDs are comprised of carbon (92.54%), oxygen (4.29%), and nitrogen (3.15%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH dependence of PL emission of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxylic (–COOH) and amine (–NH2) functionalities on the surface may provide high oxidation and reduction potentials of the CQDs. The sensing potential of CQDs is highly affected by pH (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014; Mehta et al., 2017). To explore the pH effect on sensing potential of CQDs, PL intensity for the mango leaf-derived CQDs was monitored at different pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11). As shown in Fig. 3a, under strong acidic conditions (pH = 1) or under strong alkaline conditions (pH = 11), the fluorescence intensity peaks were weak. Under strong acidic conditions, the CQD surface was favorably occupied with carboxylic acid groups (–COOH). Under such conditions, electron-hole pair recombination is obstructed, which ultimately leads to reduction in fluorescence intensity. However, in acidic conditions, a protonated form of NH3+ weakened electron-donating efficiency. In contrast, under strong basic conditions, the amine (–NH2) functionality becomes dominant on the CQDs surface to offer a high electron-donating ability. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7; thus, the whole sensing platform (CQDs suspension + water) was adjusted to a neutral pH value (7) for optimum detection of Fe2+ ions. Moreover, this pH-dependent fluorescence property of CQDs can be employed in pH-sensitive fluorescence sensors. This may provide an extra benefit for future application in the field of pH-based fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe2+ ions on PL spectra of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3b shows the plot of PL intensity against wavelength of the CQDs in the presence of Fe2+ upon excitation at a wavelength of 395 nm. Quenching in PL intensity of the CQDs was observed, which might be due to the inner filter effect: the quencher ions quench the emission of luminescent materials (Song et al., 2014). Therefore, Fe2+ ions quench the PL intensity of CQDs. Note that ground state electrons of the CQDs can be excited by absorbing sufficient energy, producing photoluminescence emission. Then, these excited electrons return to their respective ground states (known as relaxation) by releasing the absorbed energy in the form of light. However, the magnitude of emitted energy is less than that of absorbed energy, because some part of the latter is lost in the form of heat during relaxation.

The M. indica leaf-derived CQDs were tested through analysis of PL intensity for selectivity of Cu2+, Al3+, K2+, Mg2+, Li2+, Na+, Mn2+, Co2+ Zn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, and Fe2+ ions (Fig. 3b). Metal ions on the CQDs absorbed the energy of the excited-state electrons, resulting in relaxation of the excited electrons to ground state without photon emission. This phenomenon is known as luminescence quenching. The effect of Fe2+ ion concentration on PL intensity of the CQDs was further explored by testing 10 different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3c, the PL intensity of CQDs decreased with an increase in Fe2+ ion concentration from 0 μL to 1000 μL (10 ppm Fe2+ solution). This implies that the PL intensity of the CQDs was dependent on concentration of Fe2+ ions. The PL intensity decreased with an increase in concentration of Fe2+ ions, meaning that the Fe2+ ions behaved as a quencher of luminescence in the CQDs, or luminescence of CQDs is sensitive to Fe2+ ions. The interaction of Fe2+ metal ions with the –OH, –NH2, and –COOH groups present on the CQD surface also led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Hence, the energy was released in the form of non-radiative recombination (Wang et al., 2019; Yarur et al., 2019). The effects of Fe2+ ion concentration on photoluminescence intensity of CQDs as well as the Stern-Volmer plot for sensing of Fe2+ ions are shown in Fig. 3d. The limit of detection (LOD) can be estimated by the following equation:
LOD =
3 × standard deviation
slope
The LOD of the synthesized CQDs was calculated to be 0.662 ppm with a standard deviation of R 2 = 0.95445.
The photoluminescent quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was compared with a standard reference sample (quinine sulphate). Quinine sulphate was dissolved in 0.01 M H2SO4.

QY was calculated at an excitation wavelength of 435 nm as follows:
Q
Y s
= Q
Y R
×
η R 2
η S 2
×
I S
A S
×
A R
I R
where I is total intensity of photoluminescence, A is absorbance, and η is index of refraction. The R subscript represents reference, and S subscript represents sample. The QY of the CQDs was calculated to be ~18.2%. The LOD of other photoluminescent materials is depicted in Table 1 . It was observed that the LOD in the present work is high relative to others, which validates the efficacy of this study. Moreover, in most previous studies, CQDs were synthesized using highly reactive chemicals and equipment at high cost. Additionally, many dopants (e.g., nitrogen) were incorporated into CQDs to enhance their sensing potential. However, in this work, CQDs were synthesized via a facile, eco-friendly, and cost-effective approach using a renewable carbon source (mango leaves), which turned out to have a remarkable sensing potential for iron ions in water.

To validate the efficacy of the developed sensing platform, a Livogen tablet (composed of ferrous fumarate (152 mg)) was tested as a real sample in this study. Various doses of the Livogen tablet were used for preparation of solutions. Fig. 4 shows that PL intensity of the synthesized CQDs decreases with an increase in concentration of Fe2+, which indicates the effective sensing potential of the prepared CQDs. The same relationship is shown in Fig. 3c, in which the PL intensity of the CQDs decreased with an increase in concentration of Fe2+ ions (0 μL to 1000 μL of 10 ppm Fe2+ solution) in water. Thus, CQDs might be potential sensing materials for detection of Fe2+ metal ions.

As shown in Fig. 5 . the sensing mechanism of CQDs is based on the photoluminescence quenching and non-radiative relaxation process. By absorbing the sufficient light energy, the electronic excitation process starts in CQDs. In the absence of any metal ion, the electrons simply relax to ground state with the emission of light (e.g., known as radiative relaxation process) and gives the emission spectra in PL. However, in the presence of metal ions, the excited electrons of CQDs interact with metal ions and non-radiative relaxation process takes place. Therefore, the photoluminescence quenching was observed in PL spectra with continuous decline in the intensity of peaks.

Further, to check the stability of CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded again after three months (as depicted in Fig. 6 ). Accordingly, a minor decline in the absorbance peak without shifting the absorption band (213 nm) was observed. This observation validates the exceptional stability of the prepared CQDs over long time period, which is a very momentous property for their practical implementation at industrial level.

In this study, synthesis of photoluminescent CQDs was carried out using a renewable carbon source (M. indica leaves) via pyrolysis without pre-/post-chemical treatments. Various spectroscopic and electron microscopic techniques confirmed the spherical shape and amphiphilic nature of the CQDs in a size range of 1–5 nm. The synthesized CQDs exhibited effective sensing potential for Fe2+ ions in water and in a Livogen tablet (as a real sample), with a quantum yield of ~18.2%. The method introduced in this study can provide facile preparation of bright CQDs from abundant low-cost precursors that can then be employed for cost-effective and selective sensors for detection of metal ions.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Jagpreet Singh:Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Sukhmeen Kaur:Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.Jechan Lee:Formal analysis.Akansha Mehta:Data curation, Investigation.Sanjeev Kumar:Data curation, Investigation.Ki-Hyun Kim:Conceptualization, Supervision.Soumen Basu:Data curation, Investigation.Mohit Rawat:Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: This experimental research work is original and was carried out at the authors' institute. This work possesses no intellectual or financial conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by financial assistance from the Shromani Gurdwara Prabhandak Committee (SGPC), Amritsar. The authors are thankful to the Vice-Chancellor of SGGSW and Thapar University for providing laboratory facilities.
